events: A king ordered his three sons to seek their fortunes.
The three brothers took different directions but agreed to meet in a certain place in the forest.
Don Juan met an old man on the road who gave him bread and advised him to divide it among the monkeys guarding the palace gate.
Don Juan entered the palace and was greeted by a big monkey who told him he would marry Chonguita, the monkey's daughter.
Don Juan and Chonguita were married without delay.
Don Juan asked permission to leave, but his wife's mother insisted that she accompany him.
Don Juan met his brothers and their wives at the agreed meeting place, with Chonguita by his side.
His brothers were surprised to see a monkey as Don Juan's wife.
The king was informed of his son's marriage and fainted upon learning the news.
The king assigned houses for each couple to live in but was still displeased with Don Juan's choice.
The king ordered his daughters-in-law to make him an embroidered coat within three days, under penalty of death if they failed.
Chonguita surprised everyone by creating the most beautiful coat among them.
The king then ordered his daughters-in-law to embroider a cap for him within two days, again under penalty of death if they failed.
Chonguita's picture on the palace walls was found to be the most beautiful among them, and she was crowned queen.
A youth herded buffaloes and noticed that a she-dog visited a ravine every day at noon.
The youth hid himself to watch the dog and saw it transform into a beautiful maiden.
The youth followed the maiden to her house in the village.
The youth's father and mother decided it was time for him to marry.
The youth announced that he wanted to marry a dog instead of a human girl.
Everyone laughed at the youth's idea, but his parents eventually agreed to let him have his way.
The youth chose the man whose house the maiden visited as her potential husband.
A day was fixed for the wedding and the ceremony took place.
Every night, the bride would transform back into a dog and leave the house.
The youth discovered his bride's secret and caught her in the act of transforming.
The youth threw the dog skin into the fire, freeing his bride from its magic.
The freed bride was revealed to be a woman of extraordinary beauty.
Jitu, the youth's friend, decided to marry a dog himself after seeing his friend's success.
Jitu's marriage ended in disaster when his bride broke loose and bit him, causing him to hang himself in shame.
A monk was seated on the seashore when he saw a kite fly holding a little mouse.
The mouse escaped and fell onto the monk.
Touched by compassion, the monk took the mouse home with him.
He prayed to change the mouse into a young girl, which was immediately granted.
The monk introduced the young girl to his wife as her own child.
When the young girl reached womanhood, she asked the monk for a husband who was the most powerful in the world.
The monk suggested the sun as a potential husband, but it replied that it was not the most powerful.
The sun directed the monk to find the cloud, which obscured its rays and made them pale.
The cloud told the monk that it was not the most powerful, but rather the wind that drove it from east to west.
The wind directed the monk to find the mountain, which said that it could not move against the rat that bored into it.
The mountain told the monk that the rat was more powerful than itself.
The monk asked the rat if he would marry his daughter, but the rat replied that a rat only marries a mouse.
The monk prayed to change his daughter back into a mouse, which was immediately granted.
The mouse ran away with the rat.
The Bodhisatta came to life as a young lion.
A certain hare lived beneath a palm sapling at the foot of a vilva tree near the Western Ocean.
A ripe vilva fruit fell on a palm leaf, and the hare thought 'The earth is collapsing'.
The hare fled without looking back, and another hare saw him running and asked for the cause of his panic flight.
The first hare said 'The earth here is breaking up,' and the second hare ran after him.
One hundred thousand hares took to flight together, and other animals saw them running and asked what it meant.
When told that the earth was breaking up, they too took to flight, extending their host to a full league in length.
The Bodhisatta saw the headlong flight of animals and heard the cause of it, and thought 'The earth is nowhere coming to an end'.
He got before them to the foot of a mountain and roared three times, frightening them into stopping their flight.
The lion asked why they were running away, and they said 'The earth is collapsing'.
He questioned each animal in turn, but none knew anything about the earth breaking up.
Finally, he asked the hares if they knew, and they pointed to one particular hare who said 'Yes, I saw it'.
The lion suspected that a ripe vilva fruit had fallen on a palm leaf and caused the sound, so he took the hare with him to investigate.
He found out that the earth was not breaking up, and reassured the herd of animals before letting them go.
A farmer's wife was winnowing corn.
A crow swooped off with a grain from the winnowing basket and perched on a tree close by to eat it.
The farmer's wife flung a clod at the bird with so good an aim that the crow fell to the ground, dropping the grain of corn.
The grain of corn rolled into a crack in the tree.
The farmer's wife ran up to the crow and seized it by the tail.
She cried, 'Give me back my grain of corn, or I will kill you!'
The crow promised to do so, but could not reach the grain in the crack.
He flew off to a woodman and asked him to cut the tree.
The woodman refused to cut the tree.
The crow then flew on to the king's palace and asked him to kill the man who had not cut the tree.
The king refused to kill the man.
The crow then flew on to the queen and asked her to coax the king into killing the man.
The queen refused to coax the king.
The crow met a snake who refused to bite the queen.
Brer Rabbit was going along through the bushes singing to himself.
He saw a shadow pass before him.
He looked up, and there was Mr. Hawk sailing around and around.
Every time he sailed around, he got a little closer, but Brer Rabbit didn't notice this.
Down he dropped right slam-bang on Brer Rabbit, and there he had him.
Mr. Hawk held him in a mighty tight grip.
He hollered and he begged, but that didn't do any good.
Brer Rabbit then lay still and studied about what in the name of goodness he was going to do.
Mr. Hawk said, 'I'll do away with you, and then I'll go catch me a couple of jay-birds.'
Brer Rabbit begged Mr. Hawk to go catch the jay-birds first.
Mr. Hawk said, 'Oh-oh, Brer Rabbit, you've been fooling too many folks. You're not fooling me.'
Brer Rabbit suggested that Mr. Hawk wait and let him get tame.
Mr. Hawk said, 'Now you are talking sense!'
Brer Rabbit got loose without getting bigger or driving out partridges for Mr. Hawk.
An old man was returning home laden with firewood when he was overtaken by a terrible thunderstorm.
He sought shelter in a hollow tree.
When the storm had abated, he heard a sound of merriment close at hand.
He peeped out from his place of retreat and saw a number of demons dancing and singing and drinking.
The old man forgot caution and began to laugh at the demons' strange dance.
A demon was dancing by himself, and the chief of the company was displeased with his antics.
The leader of the demons asked if anyone could dance better than the clumsy demon.
The old man offered to show his skill as a dancer, and he danced before the gathering of demons.
The demons congratulated him on his performance and asked him to give them more dances.
The chief of the demons removed a wen from the old man's right cheek as a pledge for his return the next night.
The demons vanished, leaving the old man with his face smooth and free of the wen.
A wicked and cantankerous old man heard about the old man's good fortune and decided to try it for himself.
The wicked old man hid in the hollow tree and attempted to dance with the demons.
However, he was unable to dance well, and the demons returned his wen as a pledge, leaving him with two wens on his face.
A certain man was overtaken by darkness among the mountains.
He sought shelter in the trunk of a hollow tree.
A large company of elves assembled at the place.
The man was frightened out of his wits when he peeped out from his hiding place.
However, the elves began to feast and drink wine, and sing and dance.
The man forgot all about his fright and crept out of his hollow tree to join in the revels.
When the day was about to dawn, the elves said to the man, 'You're a very jolly companion...'
The elves took a large wen that grew on his forehead and kept it in pawn.
The man walked off to his own house in high glee at having passed a jovial night.
He got rid of his wen into the bargain.
A neighbor who was troubled with a wen heard about the man's luck and went to hunt for the hollow tree.
The elves mistook him for their former boon-companion and said, 'You're a good fellow...'
One of the elves stuck the pawned wen onto the man's forehead on top of his other wen.
A vessel from Batavia was on the point of entering Table Bay in stress of weather during the Dutch occupation of the Cape.
The batteries fired on the distressed ship and compelled it to put to sea, where it was lost.
According to sailors, the vessel has continued to beat about ever since and will continue to do so till the day of judgment.
The 'Dutchman' is said to appear generally to ships in a heavy gale with all sail set.
On April 6, 1823, His Majesty's ship Leven saw her consort the Barracouta about two miles to leeward.
The peculiar rig of the Barracouta left no doubt as to her identity, and many well-known faces were distinctly visible looking towards the Leven.
Capt. Owen attempted to close with the Barracouta to speak, but was surprised that she made no effort to join the Leven.
The next morning, the Leven anchored in Simon's Bay where for a whole week the Barracouta was anxiously expected.
On her arrival (the 14th), it was seen by her log that she was 300 miles from the Leven when the latter thought she saw her.
A similar phenomenon was witnessed by the Leven on another occasion, and a boat was apparently lowered as if to lure its victim.
The veteran sailor was not caught, and the Leven after many perils reached England in safety.
A merchant ship passenger saw a vessel in nearly similar circumstances three times: once when they hoisted lights to speak with the stranger.
The third time was on a recent return from India, where the wind blew a stiff gale and the crew made their bark all snug for the night.
Suddenly, the second officer exclaimed 'Voila le volant Hollandais!' as he ascended the weather rigging to point out the approaching ship.
She said one day to the bad one: 'Go and draw a bucket of water.'
The bad one did not want to go, and so she would not obey her mother.
The good daughter, however, said: 'I will go and draw it.'
She went to draw the water, and the bucket fell down the well.
She climbed down the well, and at the bottom found a narrow passage, with a door.
She knocked at the door. 'Have you not found a cord and bucket?'
There was a saint there, who answered: 'No, my child.'
The Madonna replied: 'Yes, my child. Listen...'
The good girl put the house in order, gave the child his broth, swept the house; and while she was sweeping, instead of finding dirt, she found coral and other beautiful things.
When the Madonna came back, she asked: 'Have you done all I told you to do?'
The bad girl went down the well, let the bucket fall, climbed down, and knocked at the saint's door.
She knocked at the Madonna's door. 'Have you not found a cord and bucket?'
The Madonna said that she had. 'I am going away: you will give my son his broth...'
Her mother began to wash her face and rub it; and the more she did so the less the dirt went away.
A Sultan had a daughter, and the daughter used to be taught the Koran.
One day the Sultan went on a pilgrimage, and entrusted his daughter to a priest and said, 'Continue to teach that girl the Koran.'
The priest coveted the girl, wishing to lie with her, but the girl refused.
One day she said, 'Come to me tomorrow'.
On the day arranged she removed from the house the ladder by which the priest used to ascend.
He then sent a letter to her father, and he wrote, 'Your daughter has become a whore.'
The Sultan returned from the pilgrimage, and he was angry with the girl, and he handed her over to some slaves,
and they cut off her legs while they dug her grave.
While they were digging the grave she crawled away, and went into some trees and hid.
A caravan passed by the place and camped where the girl lay.
The man who took the girl put her to live in a house.
The Sultan's son saw the girl's face, and he said, 'Let me marry that girl from you.'
And so the Sultan's son married her.
She bore two children, and while she was with child the young man said, 'I am going on a pilgrimage'.
A peasant and his wife had a child they refused to baptize until they could find a just man for the godfather.
The father took the child in his arms and went into the street to look for this just man.
He met our Lord, who was asked if he was just, but replied 'I don't know whether I am just'.
The peasant passed on and met the Madonna, who also said she didn't know if she was just.
He continued his search and met Death, who claimed to be just.
Death agreed to baptize the child with the peasant.
After baptism, Death led the peasant into a long room filled with many burning lights.
Death explained that the lights represented the souls of all people in the world.
The peasant saw his own light was about to expire and asked what would happen when it was gone.
Death replied that he would have to come with him, as Death is inevitable.
The peasant begged to take some oil from his son's lamp to put in his own, but Death refused.
Death told the peasant it was time for him to go home and arrange his affairs.
Death stated that he was waiting for the peasant, implying their inevitable meeting.
A little girl named Katie was passing by an old woman's house.
The old woman invited Katie to come in and offered her apples to eat.
After eating the apples, the old woman asked Katie to stay with her all the time.
Katie remembered that she had an errand to get something for supper from her mother.
The old woman took a wand and hit the table, causing good things to appear in Katie's mother's house.
Katie agreed to stay with the old woman for a week.
At the end of the week, Katie had a dream that an elf warned her the witch would kill her.
The next night, Katie dreamed the same thing and was given a comb and handkerchief by the elf.
Katie jumped out of the window in the middle of the night to escape the witch.
As she fled, Katie dropped her comb, causing a forest to grow up and block the witch's path.
After crossing the forest, Katie dropped her handkerchief, creating a river that the witch had to cross.
The witch was eventually beheaded by a woodcutter in a nearby woods.
The woodcutter took Katie home safely.
Katie's mother scolded her for disobeying and warned about the consequences of disobedience.
Brer Wolf went into the house while Brer Rabbit and his mother were out picking salad for dinner.
Brer Rabbit returned home and noticed that everything was quiet and the front door was slightly ajar.
Brer Rabbit approached the house cautiously, peeping and listening but seeing or hearing nothing suspicious.
Brer Rabbit decided to use his wit to figure out who was in the house without entering it himself.
He climbed a nearby persimmon stump and began hollering 'Heyo, House!' to try to get an answer from the house.
The house remained silent, but Brer Wolf inside the house was startled by Brer Rabbit's loud hollering.
Brer Rabbit continued to holler louder and louder, trying to get a response from the house.
Brer Wolf became increasingly agitated and restless inside the house, feeling cold chills run down his back.
Finally, Brer Wolf tried to mimic the sound of a house by hollering 'Heyo, yo'se'f!' in a hoarse voice.
Brer Rabbit was amused by Brer Wolf's attempt and laughed before dropping off the persimmon stump and hurting himself.
Uncle Remus continued the story, saying that after this incident, Brer Wolf slunk out of the house and made a break for home.
Brer Rabbit lived a long time without any other creatures bothering him again.
A man went into the forest to gather wood.
He saw a snake crushed under a large stone.
The man raised the stone with his axe handle and freed the snake.
The snake threatened to eat the man in return for saving its life.
The man suggested that they seek a judge's opinion before deciding what to do.
They met a thin horse tied to an oak tree who was famished and eating leaves.
The snake asked the horse if it was right to eat the man who had saved its life.
The horse agreed that the snake should eat the man because he would be doing a good deed.
They met a mulberry tree that was eaten by old age and asked for its opinion.
The tree also agreed that the snake should eat the man because it had been well rewarded in life.
They then met a fox who the man begged to pronounce judgment in his favor.
The fox asked to see how the situation had happened and they returned to the original scene.
The man tricked the snake by pretending it was still under the stone, and left it there.
The fox was tricked into eating dogs instead of hens in a bag, and was eaten herself.
A crocodile made his burrow in the embankment of a tank near a village.
The mud dried and became hard, so the crocodile was unable to get out of the hole.
He was going to die.
A man passed by on his way to fetch a midwife to attend to his wife.
The crocodile heard him and said, 'Save me by breaking up the earth, so that I may get out.'
The man broke up the earth and let him out.
As there was no water left in the tank, the man placed the crocodile on his shoulder and went to the edge of the river.
He then placed the crocodile into the water, but no sooner had he done so than the crocodile seized him by the arm and was about to eat him.
The man said, 'It is good. You may eat me, but first let us ask two or three persons as witnesses.'
They came to a kumbuk tree, and the man said to it, 'This crocodile is going to eat me. I am asking your opinion of it.'
The kumbuk tree said, 'O crocodile, do not let that man go. There is no animal so wicked as that man.'
He went and asked a cow about the situation.
The cow said, 'O crocodile, do not let that man go. That man is a wicked man. He takes our milk, and in the end he kills and eats us.'
He then asked a jackal about it.
A lion was trapped in a rocky cleft.
The lion asked a horse for help to be freed.
The horse agreed to help the lion on one condition: not to eat him.
The horse dug with his hooves until he had freed the lion.
As soon as the lion was free, he said he would eat the horse.
The horse asked the lion to recall their agreement not to eat each other.
The lion refused, and the horse suggested going before a judge.
They agreed to go before the fox as the judge.
The fox asked them to return to the rocky cleft to determine their original positions.
The lion was unable to fit back into his original position due to being too fat.
The fox declared that the lion should be trapped in the cleft again.
The horse refused to help the lion out of the cleft and instead threw stones at him until he died.
The farmer was terrified.
He unhitched one of the oxen and gave it to the lion.
The lion took it and dragged it away.
The farmer went home with the remaining ox and bought a second one that same evening so that the next day he would be able to plow again.

The next day the farmer plowed again from morning until evening, and when it was evening the lion came again and said, 'Farmer, give me one of your two oxen or I'll kill both of them and you as well.'
Again the farmer gave him an ox.
That evening he bought another ox so that he would be able to plow again the next day.
One evening the jackal came by as the farmer was driving his single ox home.
The jackal said, 'Every morning I see you leave the farmyard with two oxen and every evening I see you coming back with only one. Why is that?'
The farmer said, 'Every evening when I am finished with the day's work the lion comes and demands one of my oxen and threatens to kill me and both oxen if I don't grant his wish.'
The jackal said, 'If you promise to give me a sheep I will free you from the lion.'
The farmer answered, 'If you can free me from the lion I will gladly promise you a sheep.'

The jackal said, 'Tomorrow I will call out with a disguised voice from up there on the hill and ask who is speaking with you. Then you answer that it is only an Asko (a block of wood to be split). Have a hatchet at hand. Do you understand me?'
The jackal came down from the hill and said, 'I have done what I promised. The lion is finished. Tomorrow I will come again to fetch the sheep that you promised me.'
A king was rearing wild animals.
The king had learnt in a thorough manner the speech of animals.
The fowls were saying, 'Our king assists us very much; he gives us food and drink.'
The royal queen asked, 'What did you laugh at?'
The king said, 'I merely (nikan) laughed,' without explaining the reason.
The royal queen says, 'There is no one who laughs in that way without a reason. Should you not say the reason I am going away, or having jumped into a well I shall die.'
A cock said, 'His majesty our king is going to be lost. We don't want the food. We shall not receive assistance.'
The queen asked, 'What did you laugh at?' again.
The king said, 'Thinking of constructing a tank, I laughed.'
The queen suggested building a tank with the animals' help.
The king showed the animals a place to build the tank and left them to work on it.
After three or four days, the king returned to find the jackal looking on while the other animals worked.
The king asked the jackal why he was not working, and the jackal said he was checking an account.
The jackal explained that according to his count, females were in excess in the country.
There were three brothers.
Two had no clothes and one no shirt.
The weather was very bad.
They made up their minds to go shooting.
They took down three guns -- two were broken and one had no barrel -- and walked and walked until they came at last to a meadow.
There, they saw a hare.
They began to fire at it, but could not catch it.
One of them said, 'What shall we do?'
They remembered that nearby a godmother of theirs lived.
So they went and knocked at her door and asked her to lend them a pot to cook the hare they had not caught.
The godmother was not at home, but she answered from inside, 'My children, go in the kitchen and there you will find three pots...'
They went into the kitchen and chose the one without a bottom and put the hare in it to cook.
One said, 'Let's ask our godmother whether she has anything in her garden.'
The godmother said, 'I have three walnut trees. Two are dead and one has never borne any nuts. Knock off as many as you wish.'
A dragon living in the great sea saw that his wife's health was not good.
He asked his wife what he could get her to eat.
Mrs. Dragon was silent and refused to tell him.
The dragon pleaded with his wife to tell him what she wanted to eat.
She finally told him that she wanted a monkey's heart to eat.
The dragon went on shore and spotted a monkey on top of a tree.
He called out to the monkey and asked if he was afraid of falling from the tree.
The monkey replied that he had no fear of falling.
The dragon offered to take the monkey across the sea to find more fruit and flowers.
The monkey got on the dragon's back, but the dragon suddenly dived down into the water.
The monkey asked where they were going, and the dragon explained that his wife was ill and wanted a monkey's heart to eat.
The monkey realized he had left his heart on the tree and offered to get it for Mrs. Dragon.
The dragon returned to the shore with the monkey.
As the monkey was tardy in coming down from the tree, the dragon scolded him to hurry up.
The parents of a prince wanted him to marry.
He said, 'I will marry only such a woman about whom I can say with good conscience that she is the most sensitive woman in the world.'
His parents answered, 'Then go and find her!'
He went forth and came to a woman whose head was all bandaged up and who appeared to be suffering.
The prince asked the woman, 'What is the matter with you?'
She said, 'This morning my maid was combing my hair, and she pulled one out, which is causing me great pain.'
The prince thought to himself, 'She isn't the right one. I will seek further.'
He went on his way and found another woman.
Her entire body was wrapped with the finest linen, and she looked very sad.
The prince asked her, 'What is the matter with you?'
She said, 'Last night while I was in bed there was a little wrinkle in the sheet I was lying on, and it has made me sick.'
The prince thought, 'She is not the most sensitive one either. There must be a better one.'
He continued on his way and came to a third woman.
She was sitting in an easy chair and had a bandaged foot.
Sennuccio and his wife Bedovina were seated near the door of their house to take the air.
Sennuccio asked Bedovina to shut the door, but she refused.
They made a bargain that whoever spoke first would have to shut the door.
Neither Sennuccio nor Bedovina dared to speak for fear of shutting the door.
Bedovina grew heavy with sleep and went to bed, leaving Sennuccio sitting on a bench.
A serving-man passed by their house and asked if anyone was inside to give him a light for his candle.
The servant entered the house and found Sennuccio sitting with his eyes open on the bench, but he refused to speak.
The servant went further into the house and found Bedovina lying alone in bed, but she also refused to speak.
The servant lay down beside Bedovina and took advantage of her silence.
When the young man had gone, Bedovina got out of bed and found Sennuccio still awake by the door.
Bedovina scolded Sennuccio for leaving her alone with the door open, but he replied that she should have shut it herself since she spoke first.
Bedovina realized she had lost the wager and shut the door, then went to bed with Sennuccio.
A poor farmer and his wife finished their day's labor and ate their supper.
A dispute arose between them as to who should shut the door, which had been blown open by a gust of wind.
The man said 'Wife, shut the door!'
The woman replied 'Husband, shut it yourself!'
The husband proposed that the one who spoke the first word should shut the door.
The wife agreed to this proposal and they retired to bed in silence.
In the middle of the night, a wild dog entered their room and devoured their food.
The couple remained silent despite the noise made by the dog.
The next morning, the woman took some grain to have it ground into flour at her neighbor's house.
While she was away, a barber entered and asked the husband how he was sitting alone.
The farmer remained silent in response to the barber's question.
The barber shaved half of the farmer's head, beard, and mustache without getting a response from him.
The barber then covered the farmer with lampblack, but he still remained silent.
The barber left in haste, thinking the man was bewitched.
The wife returned from the mill and saw her husband's ghastly appearance.
She trembled and exclaimed 'Ah! wretch, what have you been doing?'
The farmer replied 'You spoke the first word, so begone, woman, and shut the door.'
A husband and a wife were living together.
The husband said to his wife, 'Let us have some fritters.'
She replied, 'What shall we do for a frying pan?'
He told her to go and borrow one from his godmother.
The wife went to borrow the pan, which was only a little way off.
When she returned, she said to her husband, 'Here is the pan, but you must carry it back.'
They cooked the fritters and then decided to work together to win a wager.
The husband was a shoemaker and began drawing his thread while his wife spun.
A soldier with a horse passed by their house and asked if there was a shoemaker in the street.
The soldier's wife took him to the house, where he asked the shoemaker to cut his horse a girth.
The shoemaker only replied with 'Leulerò, leulerò,' and his wife said 'Piciciì, picicì, piciciò'.
The soldier grew angry and threatened to cut the shoemaker's head off if he didn't comply.
The shoemaker remained silent, and his wife cried out for mercy when she saw the soldier seize her husband's head.
The husband finally spoke up and said, 'Now you go and carry the pan back to my godmother, and I will go and cut the horse's girth.'
A frightful drought occurred.
The rivers dried up and springs gave no water.
Animals gathered to discuss the problem.
One suggested dancing in the dry riverbed to tread out water.
The animals danced and ultimately danced the water to the surface.
Rabbit refused to dance but later drank from the riverbed.
The other animals discovered Rabbit's footprints in the ground.
Tortoise suggested catching Rabbit by covering his shell with pitch and lying down near the water.
Rabbit stepped on Tortoise, but his feet stuck to the pitch.
Tortoise carried Rabbit back to the other animals.
The animals decided that Rabbit must die for not following their plan.
Rabbit requested a shameful death by being taken by his tail and dashed against a stone.
Lion was chosen to kill Rabbit, but he ended up freeing him instead.
Brer Rabbit could never get any peace due to Brer Wolf making raids on his family.
Brer Rabbit built a straw house, but it was torn down by Brer Wolf.
Brer Rabbit then made a pine top house, which also got destroyed.
He then built a bark house, but that too was raided by Brer Wolf.
Each time his house was destroyed, one of his children was taken by Brer Wolf.
Brer Rabbit got angry and decided to build a plank house with a rock foundation.
The new house provided peace and quiet for Brer Rabbit and his family.
He built a hole in the cellar where his children could hide when there was trouble.
Brer Wolf laid low after seeing the new house, but the little rabbits were still skittish.
One day, Brer Wolf ran into the house, covered in mud and out of breath, seeking refuge from dogs.
Brer Rabbit hid Brer Wolf in a chest and pretended to make him tea and provide comfort.
However, Brer Rabbit secretly bored holes in the chest lid for air and added more wood to the fire to keep Brer Wolf warm.
Finally, Brer Rabbit poured scalding water onto the chest lid, killing Brer Wolf.
Brer Rabbit then called in his neighbors and they held a jubilee to celebrate their victory over Brer Wolf.
Hlakanyana met a boy tending some goats.
The boy had a digging-stick with him.
Hlakanyana proposed that they should pursue after birds, and the boy agreed.
They pursued birds the whole day.
In the evening, when the sun set, Hlakanyana said, 'It is time now to roast our birds.'
Hlakanyana said, 'We must go under the water and see who will come out last.'
They went under the water, and Hlakanyana came out last.
The boy agreed to try again, and they went under the water.
Hlakanyana came out quickly and ate all the birds, leaving only their heads.
Hlakanyana said, 'You have eaten them, because you came out of the water first, and you have left me the heads only.'
The boy denied having done so, but Hlakanyana said, 'You must pay for my birds with that digging-stick.'
Hlakanyana lent the digging-stick to some people making pots of clay.
The first time they stuck it in the clay, it broke.
Hlakanyana said, 'You have broken my digging-stick, the digging-stick that I received from my companion...'
A hermit found a large cave hidden from view while walking in a desert place.
The hermit saw something shining brightly inside the cave and realized it was gold.
The hermit immediately left the cave and began to run through the desert as fast as he could go.
The hermit met three great ruffians who haunted that wild place to rob travelers.
The ruffians stopped the hermit to ask why he was fleeing, and he replied that Death was chasing him.
The hermit led the ruffians to the cave, where they saw the gold and realized it was Death.
The three ruffians congratulated themselves on finding the wealth and dismissed the hermit.
The ruffians decided to stay together to guard the wealth and figure out what to do with it.
One of the ruffians suggested taking some gold to sell in the city for supplies, but another had a different plan.
The devil put an evil thought into one of the ruffians' hearts to poison his companions and take all the wealth for himself.
The ruffian who was sent to the city poisoned the food he brought back to his companions.
When the poisoned food was eaten by the two remaining ruffians, they died immediately.
The hermit returned to the cave and found that the three ruffians had all died, leaving the gold untouched.
A husband and a wife lived together.
The husband was a tailor, and his wife was also a tailor as well as a good housekeeper.
One day the husband found some things in the kitchen broken: pots, glasses, plates.
He asked his wife how the items were broken.
The wife replied 'How do I know?' when questioned by her husband.
The husband pressed for an answer, asking who broke the items.
The wife, in anger, said she had broken them with the scissors.
The husband was skeptical and decided to investigate further by lowering his wife into a well.
He tied a rope around her and began to lower her into the well, asking how she broke the items.
Despite being lowered halfway down the well, the wife continued to insist that she had broken the items with scissors.
The husband let her down further until her feet touched the water and still she insisted on using scissors.
He then let her down into the water to her waist, but she continued to deny breaking the items with anything else.
Enraged by her obstinacy, he let go of the rope and his wife fell under the water.
The husband realized too late that she was trying to communicate through hand gestures, indicating that she had broken the items with scissors or shears.
A king with three stalwart sons: Pedro, Diego, and Juan, decreed that his three sons should set forth into the world to seek their fortunes.
Don Juan chanced upon an old man on the road who offered him a loaf of bread and bade him proceed to a palace.
The old man instructed Don Juan to divide the bread among the monkeys who guarded the entrance to the palace.
Don Juan entered the palace gates and was met by a mighty monkey who spoke in a voice that rumbled like thunder.
The monkey declared Don Juan's intention of marrying his daughter Chonguita, and they were wedded forthwith.
Don Juan requested permission from his new bride to depart for the forest meeting place where he had agreed to meet his brothers.
Chonguita's mother overheard Don Juan's words and said that if he was departing, Chonguita should come with him.
Don Juan's brothers were astonished by his presence at the meeting place, accompanied by a monkey wife.
The king fainted from shock upon learning that one of his sons had married a monkey, but later decreed that their wives should create embroidered coats for him within three days' time.
Chonguita proved to be a skilled needlewoman and created the most exquisite coat, causing the king's heart to swell with dismay.
The king next commanded his daughters-in-law to create caps for him within two days' time, under penalty of death if they failed.
Chonguita once more surpassed her sisters-in-law in skill and beauty with the cap she created.
The king devised a plan to rid himself of Chonguita by commanding his daughters-in-law to create the most wondrous picture on the walls of his chamber within three days' time.
Chonguita's masterpiece was the most resplendent of all, and Don Juan was crowned king in a grand ceremony.
A king with three sons: Pedro, Diego, and Juan, lived in the village of Azul.
The king called his sons to him and said they should go forth from their kingdom to seek their fortunes.
The three brothers set out on their journey, each taking a different path, but agreed to meet in a certain place deep within the forest.
Don Juan came upon an old man sitting by the side of the road who offered him bread and warned him to divide it among the monkeys guarding the palace gate.
Don Juan followed the old man's instructions and entered the palace, where a large monkey approached him and spoke in a gentle voice, saying he would marry Don Juan's daughter Chonguita.
The archbishop of the monkeys was summoned, and Don Juan and Chonguita were married without delay.
Don Juan asked permission from his wife to visit the place where he and his brothers had agreed to meet, accompanied by Chonguita.
When Don Juan arrived at the meeting place, his brothers were surprised to see him with a monkey as his companion.
The king learned of his son's marriage to a monkey and was taken aback, but said he would bear it with patience.
The king assigned a house to each couple and ordered them to make him an embroidered coat within three days.
Chonguita created the most beautiful coat of all, surprising everyone, including her husband's father.
The king issued another challenge: to embroider a cap for him within two days under penalty of death.
Chonguita again created the most exquisite work, and her picture was by far the most beautiful among all the couples' efforts.
Don Juan was crowned king, and a grand feast was held in his honor, but he became angry with Chonguita for insisting that he dance with her.
A young herdsman tended to his flock of buffalo beneath the scorching sun.
He chanced upon a curious sight: every noon, a she-dog would visit a nearby ravine with hidden pools of water.
The young herdsman decided to keep watch and uncover the truth about the mysterious creature.
He hid behind a bush and observed as the she-dog shed its furry coat, revealing a beautiful maiden beneath.
The maiden bathed in the pools, her beauty shining like the sun itself.
The young herdsman followed the she-dog as it made its way to a nearby village and entered a particular house.
He learned that the house belonged to a humble family, and his parents decided it was time for him to take a wife.
The young herdsman declared he wanted to marry the she-dog, much to the amusement of his family and friends.
His parents eventually relented, and the master of the house agreed to accept a dowry in exchange for the she-dog's hand in marriage.
The young herdsman's party arrived at the bride's house to escort their newlywed wife back to their village.
Every night, the young herdsman's wife would slip out of the dog skin and disappear into the darkness.
The young herdsman caught hold of his wife and seized the dog skin, casting it into the flames where it was reduced to ashes.
The maiden stood before him, radiant with a beauty that surpassed mortal bounds.
News of their union spread quickly through the village, and soon everyone was praising the young herdsman's wisdom in marrying a creature so divine.
In the village of Nkosi, a young man named Themba lived as a skilled herdsman.
Themba noticed a peculiar sight: every day at noon, a she-dog would make its way to a nearby ravine.
Themba observed the dog from afar and saw it transform into a beautiful maiden when it reached the pool of water.
The maiden bathed in the pool and then donned the dog's skin once more, transforming back into the she-dog.
Themba followed the dog to its home, a small hut on the outskirts of the village.
Themba announced that he wanted to have the she-dog as his wife, surprising his family with this unusual choice.
Themba's family laughed at him, but they eventually agreed to let him marry the dog if there was a particular one he wanted.
Themba named the master of the she-dog as his desired bridegroom, and the master accepted a bride's price for her.
The wedding ceremony took place in due form, with the bride still wearing the dog's skin.
Every night, when her husband slept, the bride would slip out of the dog's skin and roam free.
Themba caught hold of his wife one evening as she prepared to leave, and he seized the dog's skin, throwing it into the fire where it was reduced to ashes.
Word spread quickly through the village about Themba's wisdom in marrying a dog, and his friend Jitu thought he could do no better than marry a dog himself.
Jitu's marriage ceremony took place, but when they applied vermilion to the bride's forehead, she began to growl.
Jitu eventually gave up chasing his bride and hanged himself from a tree branch in despair.
A devout monk dwelled by the sea, renowned for his prayers being answered with ease.
He chanced upon a kite soaring above the waves, its talons grasping a tiny mouse.
The creature escaped and tumbled onto the sand before the monk's very eyes.
Moved by compassion, he gently picked it up, wrapped it in a leaf, and brought it back to his humble abode.
His wife beheld the little bundle with curiosity.
The monk explained that this was no ordinary creature, but one destined for greatness.
He prayed fervently to the Almighty to transform the mouse into a maiden of surpassing beauty.
His prayer was granted, and a lovely young girl stood before them, radiant as the morning sun.
The monk presented his new daughter to his wife, saying, 'Treat her with love and care, for she is my own child.'
When she reached the prime of life, the monk said to her, 'My dear daughter, it is time you wedded. Choose a husband who suits your noble heart.'
The young woman pondered for a moment before responding, 'I desire a husband who holds sway over all else in this world.'
The monk's eyes widened with surprise, but he replied, 'Perhaps you would like the radiant sun to be your spouse?'
He set off towards the east, where the sun shone bright, and beseeched it to take the maiden as its wife.
The rat finally agreed to marry the maiden after she transformed back into a mouse.
A revered monk lived in a small village nestled between two great mountains.
The monk's prayers were always answered with haste and precision.
A magnificent kite soared above the monk, its strings dancing in the breeze.
Attached to one of the kite's claws was a tiny mouse that had fallen onto the monk's lap.
The monk picked up the mouse and wrapped it in a large leaf, cradling it as if it were a precious jewel.
The monk prayed to the Great Spirit above to transform the mouse into a young maiden of great beauty.
Instantly, the mouse was transformed into a lovely young girl with skin as smooth as jade and hair as black as the night sky.
The monk presented the young girl to his wife, saying she was their child whom he had brought home to be cared for by her.
As the years passed, the young girl grew into a beautiful woman with skin as radiant as the full moon and hair that cascaded down her back like a waterfall.
The monk approached the young girl and said it was time for her to choose a husband who suited her tastes.
The young girl requested a husband with the greatest power in all the world, and the monk suggested marrying the great Dragon King himself.
However, the dragon revealed that it was not the most powerful being in all the world, pointing out a majestic cloud as an example.
The cloud replied that it too was not the most powerful, and pointed out the Wind God as an example.
Finally, the monk discovered that the true master of power resided within the earth itself – a cunning rat who burrowed deep into the mountainside.
The Bodhisatta took on the form of a majestic young lion and dwelled in a forest realm.
A humble hare resided beneath a young palm sapling, its trunk entwined with the branches of a vilva tree.
The hare lay down to rest beneath the palm's shade and thought 'If this earth were to crumble and fall, where would I flee?'
A ripe vilva fruit dropped onto a palm leaf, startling the hare and sending him fleeing in terror.
Another hare chanced upon his frantic departure and asked the cause of his terror.
The first hare revealed that he thought the earth was breaking apart, but refused to elaborate further.
One hundred thousand hares joined in pursuit, and soon other animals such as deer, boar, elk, buffalo, wild ox, rhinoceros, tiger, lion, and elephant became aware of the commotion.
The Bodhisatta realized that some misapprehension must have sparked the panic and decided to intervene.
He positioned himself before the fleeing animals at the foot of a mountain and let out three mighty roars, causing them to halt their flight in terror.
The Bodhisatta approached the animals and asked why they fled in such terror, but each group deflected responsibility onto another.
The deer confessed that they did not know what was happening, and the hares claimed to have been informed by one of their own.
The Bodhisatta asked the accused hare to reveal what he had seen, and the hare explained how he heard the earth shattering beneath a palm sapling.
The Bodhisatta realized that a ripe vilva fruit must have fallen onto a palm leaf, producing a loud thud, and not an actual calamity.
He gently took the hare on his back and returned to the gathered animals, explaining that no calamity had befallen the earth and releasing them from their terror.
A young lion was born from the earth itself in a nearby forest.
The lion grew strong and fierce, roaming the woods as king of beasts and lord of the wild.
Near the Western Ocean, a humble hare lived under the shade of a young palm sapling at the foot of a vilva tree.
The hare thought to himself, 'If this earth were to be destroyed, where would I flee?'
A ripe vilva fruit fell on a palm leaf with a loud thud, causing the hare's heart to skip a beat.
The hare fled in terror without looking back, leaving behind his tranquil life.
A mighty host of animals joined the chase, including deer, boar, elk, buffalo, wild ox, rhinoceros, tiger, lion, and elephant.
The Bodhisatta, future Buddha, watched from afar as the chaos unfolded with keen eyes.
The Bodhisatta approached the animals and roared three times to stop them in their tracks.
The Bodhisatta questioned the animals about the cause of their panic, but each group passed the buck to another.
The deer pointed to a particular hare and said he was the one who told them the earth was breaking apart.
The Bodhisatta took the hare with him to investigate the cause of their panic near the Western Ocean.
At the palm grove, the Bodhisatta discovered that a ripe vilva fruit had fallen on a palm leaf, causing the loud thud.
The Bodhisatta returned to the herd and told them the truth, saying 'Do not be afraid; the earth is safe.'
A farmer's wife winnowed corn with diligent hands.
A cunning crow swooped down from the sky and snatched a grain from the basket.
The good woman hurled a clod of earth that struck the bird with deadly aim.
The crow tumbled to the ground, dropping its prize as it fell.
The grain rolled into a narrow crevice in the tree trunk, where it lodged out of reach.
The farmer's wife seized the fallen bird by the tail and demanded that it return her stolen grain.
The wretched crow promised to retrieve the grain but was unable to do so.
The crow flew off to seek aid from a woodsman who dwelled in a nearby glade.
The woodman refused to lend his axe to cut the tree.
The crow winged its way to the king's palace, hoping to persuade him to slay the man.
His Majesty would not be swayed, and the bird was forced to continue its quest.
The crow next sought out the queen, who referred the matter to a snake in the castle walls.
The serpent refused to bite the queen, and thus the crow flew on.
At last, the crow encountered a cat, who gave chase with unyielding ferocity when hearing the word 'mouse'.
A farmer's wife was busy winnowing corn in the village of Azizi.
A crow snatched a grain from her basket and flew off to perch on an acacia tree.
The farmer's wife flung a clod at the crow, striking it with precision and causing it to tumble to the ground.
The grain fell into a narrow crack in the tree trunk, out of reach for the crow.
The crow promised to retrieve the grain after being threatened by the farmer's wife.
When the crow returned, it found that the grain had rolled too far into the crack.
The crow flew off to seek help from the village elder, who refused to cut down the sacred tree.
The crow visited the king's palace, but the monarch refused to execute an innocent man.
The crow met a snake that refused to bite the queen and a stick that refused to beat the snake.
It encountered fire that refused to burn the stick, water that refused to quench the flames, an ox that refused to drink the water, and a rope that wouldn't bind the ox.
The crow finally met a mouse, which was too small to gnaw through the rope.
When it heard its arch-nemesis' name, the cat pounced on the mouse with lightning speed.
Brer Rabbit wandered through the underbrush, singing a merry tune to himself.
A shadow flitted across his path, and he looked up to behold Mr. Hawk soaring overhead.
Mr. Hawk's wings beat steadily as he circled closer and closer, until Brer Rabbit felt the rush of air from his wings.
Brer Rabbit was suddenly snatched from the ground by Mr. Hawk's talons.
Mr. Hawk held him fast in his talons, squeezing tight enough to make Brer Rabbit's breath come short.
Brer Rabbit cried out for mercy, but Mr. Hawk would not be swayed.
Brer Rabbit begged and pleaded, but it was all in vain.
Mr. Hawk said he would dispatch Brer Rabbit forthwith and then set his sights on a pair of jay-birds.
Brer Rabbit exclaimed that Mr. Hawk should catch the jay-birds first, as their presence grated upon his nerves.
Mr. Hawk's expression turned skeptical, and he said Brer Rabbit was trying to deceive him again.
Brer Rabbit proposed that Mr. Hawk wait until he had grown tame before eating him.
If that did not suffice, Brer Rabbit offered to grow big and strong so he could provide a full feast for Mr. Hawk.
Mr. Hawk finally relented, distracted by Brer Rabbit's proposals.
Brer Rabbit managed to wriggle free from his grasp and make good his escape.
Br'er Rabbit strolled along in a sun-dappled clearing, humming a merry tune.
He noticed a shadow flitting about above him.
Mr. Hawk swooped down, talons extended, and snatched Br'er Rabbit from his path.
Br'er Rabbit found himself ensnared in Mr. Hawk's vice-like grip.
He cried out for mercy, but it was of no avail.
Br'er Rabbit lay still, pondering his predicament.
He suggested to Mr. Hawk that he would make a poor meal and that jay-birds would be better prey.
Mr. Hawk's eyes narrowed at Br'er Rabbit's suggestion.
Br'er Rabbit proposed waiting for him to calm down or grow larger before eating him.
Mr. Hawk found himself swayed by Br'er Rabbit's arguments and agreed to wait.
However, Br'er Rabbit had no intention of growing larger or keeping his promise.
He waited until Mr. Hawk let his guard down before making his escape.
Mr. Hawk was forced to admit defeat once again.
An elderly gent found himself beset by the fury of the skies while carrying a load of firewood.
He sought refuge in a hollow tree until the tempest had passed.
As darkness began to lift, he peeped out from his sanctuary and beheld a coterie of demons dancing and singing in the moonlight.
The old man burst into laughter at the spectacle unfolding before him.
He noticed one demon who danced alone with clumsy movements, prompting the leader to scold him for lack of finesse.
The old man felt an inexplicable urge to reveal his own dancing prowess and stepped forward to dance before the demons.
The demons applauded thunderously and begged him to perform several more dances.
The demon king asked the old man to return the following eve, with one condition: he must leave a pledge behind him.
The demon king made the wen on the old man's right cheek vanish into thin air, and the demons vanished as well.
The old man shared his tale with his wife upon arriving home, who listened with a mixture of awe and trepidation.
Their neighbor, a cantankerous old man, heard the story and called upon the old man to learn more about the demons' powers.
The wicked old man set out to deceive the demons and claim their power for himself by hiding in the hollow tree just before sunset.
He emerged from his hiding place, flourishing a fan in an attempt to dance, but was ridiculed by the demons for his clumsiness.
The demon king flung the wen at the old man's right cheek, where it remained firmly fixed, and the wicked old man slunk away with two wens on either side of his face.
An elderly man named Kofi returned home from gathering firewood under the darkening sky.
The first droplets of rain began to fall, drumming against Kofi's skin like a thousand tiny fingers.
Kofi sought refuge in a hollow tree, its gnarled branches twisted like a wise elder's hands.
When the tempest had spent itself, Kofi emerged from his sanctuary, blinking away the raindrops.
Kofi heard the sound of laughter and music carried on the wind, coming from a group of demons dancing around a fire.
The leader of the demons beckoned Kofi to join in their dance party.
Kofi felt a spark ignite within him, and he offered to show the demons what he could do.
Kofi danced with a ferocity that sent shivers down the spines of the demons, who cheered and whistled in approval.
The leader of the demons invited Kofi to return the next night and dance again, but demanded a pledge from him in exchange for his newfound fame.
Kofi agreed to the demons' demand, and they removed the wen on his right cheek without causing him any pain.
The next day, Kofi's wife was overjoyed by her husband's newfound confidence and the strange tale he told of the demons' dance party.
Their neighbor, Kwame, listened with a mixture of envy and malice, and begged Kofi to take him to the hollow tree to dance for the demons.
Kwame's true intentions were revealed when he tried to deceive the demons instead of dancing, but they saw through his ruse and punished him by returning the wen to his right cheek.
A poor wight found himself lost and alone in the mountains.
He stumbled upon a hollow tree to seek refuge from the tempests.
The man huddled in the darkness as elves gathered at the tree's base.
Their laughter and music echoed through the night, drawing the man out of his hiding place.
He laughed and sang with abandon among the elves' circle of dancers and musicians.
When dawn broke, the elves bid him farewell and asked for a promise in return for their friendship.
They took a wen from his forehead as security for his pledge to return to their company.
The man returned to his village, regaling his friends with tales of his adventure and newfound freedom.
A neighbor, consumed by envy, heard of the man's luck and set out to find the hollow tree.
The envious neighbor joined the elves' revelry and was welcomed with open arms.
The elves restored the wen to his forehead, adding it to the one already there as a reminder of coveting another's good fortune.
The envious neighbor returned home in tears, his face now a map of wens.
A traveler found himself lost in the darkness of the forest.
He stumbled upon a hollow tree and sought refuge within it.
The night air was heavy with the scent of blooming flowers as he settled into his makeshift shelter.
A group of forest dwellers, known for their mischievous ways, had gathered at the tree's base and were laughing and making music.
The traveler watched with trepidation as the elves' revelry continued, but was eventually swept up in their infectious energy.
The elves welcomed the traveler with open arms and shared their wine and food with him.
As the first light of dawn crept over the horizon, the elves bid the traveler farewell but extracted a promise from him to return and join in their festivities once more.
The elves took something precious from the traveler as collateral for his promise: a wen that had grown on his forehead.
Word of the traveler's encounter spread quickly through the village, and soon he was sharing tales of his adventure with friends and family.
A neighbor who had been envious of the traveler's luck from the start saw an opportunity to replicate his experience.
The neighbor set out to find the hollow tree, determined to claim its magic for himself.
When he finally stumbled upon it, the elves welcomed him with open arms and returned the pawned wen.
Instead of removing the traveler's original wen, they stuck it onto his forehead alongside the one he already had.
A vessel from Batavia was bound for Table Bay.
The captain steered his ship through treacherous waters, but fate had other plans.
The winter season descended upon the land, and no vessel was allowed to enter the bay.
The batteries fired their cannons, and the ship was forced to put to sea once more.
His Majesty's ship Leven encountered this phantom vessel off Point Danger in 1823.
The Barracouta, a ship that should have been sailing in a different direction, was seen bearing down upon the Leven with all sail set.
Captain Owen attempted to close with the Barracouta, but she stood away from us, as if beckoning us to follow.
The next morning, the Leven anchored in Simon's Bay, waiting anxiously for the Barracouta's arrival.
When the Barracouta finally appeared on the horizon, her log revealed that she had been 300 miles away from us when we thought we saw her.
Years later, I found myself on a merchant ship, sailing through treacherous waters once more.
The second officer suddenly pointed out to sea and exclaimed 'Voila le volant Hollandais!'
The captain sent for his night glass, and soon we could see a ship bearing down upon us with all sail set.
In an instant, the stranger seemed to near us with the rapidity of lightning, but then vanished into thin air.
Mei's mother instructed her to draw a bucket of water from the well.
Mei's sister refused to help Mei with the task.
Mei volunteered to complete the task and climbed down into the well after the bucket.
Mei encountered a door where a wise old sage replied that he had not found a cord and bucket.
Mei continued on her way and met a mischievous spirit who also claimed to have not found the cord and bucket.
Mei knocked at the next door where she met the benevolent Bodhisattva.
The Bodhisattva instructed Mei to care for her son and sweep the house in exchange for the cord and bucket.
Mei completed the tasks and was rewarded with treasures by the Bodhisattva upon her return.
The Bodhisattva gave Mei a silk dress instead of calico as she had requested.
Mei received a silver thimble and was given back her bucket and cord by the Bodisattva.
A brilliant star descended upon Mei's brow when she looked up into the air at the end of the passage.
Mei returned home where her mother scolded her for being away so long.
The Bodhisattva's blessing was revealed to Mei's family when they saw the star on Mei's forehead.
Mei's sister descended into the well and met with the same doors as Mei, but lied about completing her tasks and was punished by receiving a lump of dirt instead of a star.
A wise woman lived in a village with two daughters as different as night and day.
Their mother asked the bad daughter to fetch a bucket of water from the well, but she refused.
The good daughter volunteered to go despite knowing it would be a difficult task.
As she descended into the well, the bucket slipped from her hands and fell to the bottom.
She found a narrow passage with three doors at the bottom of the well.
The first door belonged to a wise and kind saint, who replied that he had not seen her bucket and cord.
The second door was answered by a sly and wicked devil, who snarled at the good girl for being so obedient.
When she knocked on the third door, it swung open to reveal the Madonna herself.
The Madonna asked the good girl to help her with household tasks while she was away.
As she swept the floor, the good girl found precious coral and other treasures instead of dirt.
When the Madonna returned, she gave the good girl a dress of silk instead of calico as requested.
The Madonna also gave her a silver thimble instead of brass when asked for it.
Finally, the Madonna handed over the bucket and cord, saying 'When you leave this passage, look up into the air'.
A beautiful star fell onto the good girl's forehead as she emerged from the well.
A Sultan lived in a far-off land who was just and fair.
He had a daughter who was taught the Koran by a learned priest.
The priest coveted the girl's beauty and wished to claim her for his own.
The Sultan entrusted his daughter to the priest while he went on a pilgrimage.
The priest plotted against the girl and began to make advances towards her.
The girl refused the priest's advances and said 'Come to me tomorrow'.
The priest wrote a letter to the Sultan claiming his daughter was a harlot.
The Sultan returned from his pilgrimage and condemned his daughter to death based on the priest's words.
The slaves took the girl to a secluded glade in the forest to slay her.
The girl escaped and hid among the trees while the slaves slew a gazelle instead.
A caravan found the girl sitting beneath a tree with no legs and took pity on her.
The Sultan's son fell in love with the girl and begged his father for permission to marry her.
The young couple married in secret and soon had two children.
The girl regained her legs in a dream and set out on the pilgrimage to Mecca with her family.
A wise and just Sultan lived in a far-off kingdom with his beautiful daughter.
The Sultan entrusted his daughter's education to a respected priest.
However, the priest harbored secret desires for the young princess.
She cleverly outwitted him by pretending to invite him to her chambers and removing the ladder.
The priest sent a deceitful letter to the Sultan, falsely accusing his daughter of immorality.
The Sultan ordered his loyal guards to bring the princess before him, condemning her to death.
As she lay helpless, she miraculously crawled away and hid among the trees after being amputated by the slaves.
The slaves returned to the palace with a bottle of gazelle's blood, claiming it was the princess's lifeblood.
A caravan discovered the young woman sitting under a tree, her legs miraculously restored.
The Sultan's son chanced upon the young woman while visiting the town and requested that she be given to him as his wife.
She had a prophetic dream in which two birds perched upon her restored legs after being reunited with her husband on pilgrimage.
Upon waking, she discovered that her legs had indeed grown back, and she was able to walk freely once more.
The Sultan recognized his daughter and was overcome with emotion as they neared their destination on pilgrimage.
A humble farmer and his wife had brought forth a precious child.
The father cradled his child in his arms and set out into the village to search for a wise and just man to serve as his dajia.
He came upon an old sage with a kind face, who was none other than the Jade Emperor himself.
The farmer asked the Jade Emperor if he could be the child's dajia, but the Jade Emperor replied that he wasn't certain if he possessed the necessary qualities.
The farmer continued on his way and met a gentle woman with a compassionate heart, who was the Queen Mother of the West.
He asked her to be the child's dajia, but she too replied that she didn't know if she was worthy.
The farmer pressed on and soon encountered a mysterious figure shrouded in darkness, who was none other than the Weaving Maiden's sister, the Ferrywoman of the Dead.
The Ferrywoman agreed to be the child's dajia and they performed the naming ceremony.
After the ceremony, the Ferrywoman led the farmer to a vast hall filled with lanterns that seemed to stretch on forever.
The farmer was awestruck by the sight and asked the Ferrywoman what it meant.
The Ferrywoman explained that the lamps were those of all souls in the world.
The farmer saw his own lamp and realized it was about to burn out.
He begged the Ferrywoman for a little oil from his child's lamp to sustain his own, but she refused.
The Ferrywoman told the farmer that he should make arrangements for his passing and that she would be waiting for him.
In the village of Azizi, where the sun dipped into the horizon like a ripe mango, there lived a humble farmer named Kofi and his wife, Ama.
They had a child, a precious gift from the Ancestors, but they refused to have him baptized until they found a just person to be his godfather.
Kofi took their child in his arms and set out into the bustling streets of Azizi, searching for someone with a heart full of justice.
As he walked, he met a wise elder, who was none other than the great Orisha, Nyame.
Kofi asked him, 'I seek a just person to be my child's godfather. Are you just?'
Nyame replied, 'Ah, but I am not sure if I am just. The path of justice is long and winding.'
Kofi continued his search, meeting many people along the way.
He met a woman who was as gentle as a summer breeze, but she too said, 'I don't know if I am just. But go on, for you will find someone who embodies justice.'
Kofi pressed on, until he came to a woman with skin as dark as the night sky and eyes that shone like stars.
She was Mami Wata, the goddess of life and death.
Kofi asked her, 'I have been searching for a just person to be my child's godfather. Are you just?'
Mami Wata replied, 'Yes, I believe I am just. Let us baptize your child, and then I will show you what it means to be just.'
They baptized the child, and afterwards, Mami Wata led Kofi into a vast chamber filled with lights that shone like a thousand candles.
In days of yore, in a village nestled between two great rivers, there lived a young lass named Käthe.
The villagers whispered among themselves about an ancient crone who dwelled in a nearby cottage, a woman so feared and reviled that they dared not speak her name aloud.
As Käthe passed by the cottage, the crone beckoned her inside with a gnarled finger.

The interior of the cottage was a marvel to behold: walls adorned with vibrant tapestries, shelves lined with glittering trinkets, and a fire crackling in the hearth that seemed to dance with an otherworldly light.
The crone offered Käthe a basket of succulent apples, which she devoured with relish.
As they ate, the crone proposed a most unusual arrangement: Käthe would remain by her side, forsaking all duties and obligations.
But Käthe's thoughts soon turned to her mother's summons, sent in haste for a vital ingredient to prepare supper.
She excused herself, promising to return anon. The crone, however, pressed her to stay, offering a wand that seemed to shimmer with dark magic.
With a tap of the wand upon the table, a bounty of provisions appeared at Käthe's mother's doorstep, as if conjured by unseen hands.
Käthe agreed to remain for a week, and during that time, she began to experience strange and unsettling visions.
In her dreams, an elfin figure warned her of impending doom: the crone would seek to do her harm.
The elf left behind two tokens: a delicate comb and a snowy handkerchief. Käthe was instructed to drop them when the crone came in pursuit.
A young girl named Mei lived in a small village nestled between two great mountains.
Mei was known for her curiosity and love for adventure.
One day, as she wandered through the forest, she stumbled upon an ancient hut belonging to a wise woman.
The wise woman beckoned Mei to enter her abode.
Inside, Mei marveled at the vibrant tapestries and intricate carvings adorning the walls.
The wise woman offered Mei a basket of juicy, crimson apples, which she devoured in mere moments.
Mei politely declined the wise woman's offer to stay with her permanently, citing an errand she needed to complete for her mother.
The wise woman used magic to provide Mei's family with a bounty of fresh vegetables and fragrant herbs.
Mei agreed to stay with the wise woman for a week, during which time she was treated to lavish feasts and tutelage in the mystical arts.
As the days passed, Mei began to experience vivid dreams foretelling her own demise at the hands of the wise woman.
One fateful night, as the wise woman's shadow loomed behind her, Mei dropped a delicate comb onto the forest floor.
A towering bamboo stalk sprouted from the earth, blocking the wise woman's path.
The wise woman was confronted by a churning river that had appeared in an instant.
Mei discarded a silk handkerchief, summoning a torrent of rain and hailstones that further impeded the wise woman's progress.
Br'er Wolf slipped into Br'er Rabbit's house while he and his wife were out gathering greens.
Br'er Rabbit returned home and noticed that the front door was slightly ajar.
He crept up to the house, stepping quietly, but couldn't see anything amiss.
Br'er Rabbit listened in the chimney corner and under the window, yet heard nothing out of the ordinary.
He rubbed his mustache thoughtfully and pondered how to find out who was in the house without getting into trouble himself.
Br'er Rabbit climbed up onto an apple stump and began to holler 'Heyo, House! Heyo!'.
The house remained silent, confusing Br'er Wolf who was hiding behind the door.
Br'er Rabbit shouted louder still from his perch on the apple stump, but the house continued to remain silent.
Br'er Wolf tried to mimic a house's voice and hollered back 'Heyo, yo'self!'
Br'er Rabbit laughed at Br'er Wolf's attempt and said he'd have to stand out in the rain for a long time before he could talk hoarse like a house.
Br'er Rabbit climbed down from his perch and walked away, leaving Br'er Wolf behind.
Br'er Wolf slunk out of the house as fast as his legs could carry him after Br'er Rabbit left.
Ole Miss Rabbit and little Rabs ventured out to gather sallid for dinner.
Brer Wolf had slipped into their humble abode, waiting patiently for Brer Rabbit's return.
As the hours ticked by, Brer Rabbit grew weary from his journey, but his keen senses remained alert.
Upon approaching the house, he noticed an unsettling stillness, like a tranquil pond reflecting the beauty of nature.
The front door, usually secured with a latch, now creaked slightly on its hinges, sending a shiver down Brer Rabbit's spine.
Brer Rabbit crept closer, his footsteps as light as a leaf rustling in the breeze.
He peered through the crack between the door and the frame, but saw nothing out of the ordinary.
Brer Rabbit retreated a short distance from the house and climbed an 'simmon stump, where he began to holler at the top of his lungs, 'Heyo, House! Why not respond?'
The house remained silent, its stillness unbroken.
Brer Rabbit continued to holler, his voice carrying far and wide, until Brer Wolf responded with a growl, 'Heyo, yo'se'f!'
Brer Rabbit realized his opponent's attempt at mimicry and exclaimed, 'Ha! Brer Wolf, that won't do.'
The little rabbit leapt off the 'simmon stump, laughing so hard that he stumbled and nearly fell.
As the sun dipped below the horizon, casting the forest in a warm orange glow, Brer Rabbit's laughter echoed through the trees.
In a small village nestled between two great mountains, there lived a humble woodcutter named Kaito.
One day, while gathering firewood in the forest, he stumbled upon a serpent crushed beneath a massive boulder.
With gentle care, Kaito lifted the stone with his axe handle, and the snake slithered free.
As the serpent regained its strength, it gazed at Kaito with malevolent intent and hissed, 'I shall devour you'.
Kaito replied calmly, 'Let us first seek the counsel of a wise being. If I am condemned, then you may eat me as your due'.
Their search led them to a skeletal horse tied to an ancient oak tree.
The horse's eyes were sunken, its coat dull and matted. The serpent asked, 'Is it just for me to devour this man who saved my life?'
The horse replied, 'Indeed, it is more than just. Look at me! I was once a proud steed, carrying my master through many seasons. Now, I am nothing but a shadow of my former self...'
As they continued their journey, they came upon a mulberry tree, its branches gnarled and hollowed out by age.
The serpent inquired, 'Is it right to devour this man who saved my life?' The ancient tree responded without hesitation, 'Yes...'
Their next encounter was with a cunning fox. Kaito took her aside and begged for her judgment.
The fox said, 'To render a fair verdict, I must see how matters stand.' They returned to the spot where they first met...
Kaito had secretly filled the bag with dogs instead of hens. He warned her not to open it near the village.
The fox, eager for her prize, waited until they were far from human ears before opening the bag. To her dismay, the dogs burst forth and devoured her.
A man went into the woods to gather firewood for his family's hearth.
He found a serpent crushed beneath a massive stone and raised it with his axe.
The serpent gazed at the man with cold, calculating eyes and hissed that he would feast on his flesh.
The man replied calmly that they should seek the counsel of a wise one to judge whether he was condemned to be eaten.
They came upon a horse as thin as a reed, tied to an ancient oak tree, and the snake asked if it was just to devour the man who had saved its life.
The horse's voice was barely audible but said that it was right for the man to be eaten because those who do good are ill rewarded.
They continued on their journey and stumbled upon an ancient mulberry tree, which also agreed that it was right to eat the man.
The snake asked a cunning fox for her judgment, and she said she needed to see how the matter had unfolded.
The man returned the snake to its original spot under the stone, and the fox was satisfied with her judgment.
The fox requested a payment of a bag of hens from the man in exchange for her judgment.
The man promised to deliver the hens to the fox the next morning but gave her dogs instead.
The fox opened the bag and was devoured by the dogs.
The village elders say that 'who does good is ill rewarded, while who does evil reaps their just reward'.
A wily crocodile had made its lair in the embankment of a village tank.
The mud had dried and hardened, trapping the beast within.
A traveler chanced upon the scene on his way to fetch a midwife to attend to his wife's confinement.
Hearing the crocodile's plaintive cries, he inquired as to its distress.
The reptile explained its predicament and begged the man to break up the earth so that it might be freed.
Moved by compassion, the traveler obliged, loosening the hardened mud with a mighty blow of his axe.
The crocodile emerged from its prison, grateful for the stranger's kindness.
However, as they stood on the riverbank, the crocodile seized the man's arm and prepared to make a meal of him.
The traveler asked the crocodile why it intended to devour him, despite being saved from death just now.
The crocodile shrugged its scaly shoulders, explaining that hunger had taken hold and it must eat to survive.
The traveler proposed seeking the counsel of witnesses to this transaction, and they set off together.
Their first stop was beneath a venerable kumbuk tree, where the traveler asked for its opinion on the matter.
The ancient tree replied that the crocodile should not let the man go, as he was wicked and stole from it.
Undeterred, they continued to seek the counsel of a wise cow, who also advised against letting the man go.
Finally, they came to a cunning jackal, who listened intently and proposed that the traveler tell its tale from the beginning.
The jackal declared that the crocodile was wicked and should be beaten until it was dead, thanks to the traveler's kindness.
With these words, the traveler seized his club and dealt a mighty blow to the crocodile, ending its life.
Thambi was on his way to fetch a midwife for his wife, who was in labor with their first child.
As he walked along the embankment of the tank, he heard a faint cry for help from a crocodile stuck in its burrow.
Thambi freed the crocodile from its prison by breaking up the earth.
As they made their way to the river, Thambi noticed that the tank was dry.
The crocodile seized Thambi by the arm and prepared to devour him due to hunger.
Thambi asked the crocodile why it wanted to eat him after he had helped it earlier.
The crocodile explained that hunger was a mighty force that drove even wise creatures to do foolish things.
Thambi led the crocodile to a kumbuk tree and asked for its opinion on whether he should be eaten.
The kumbuk tree accused Thambi of being wicked and taking what did not belong to him.
Thambi then asked a cow for its opinion, which also accused him of being wicked and taking their milk.
The crocodile was about to eat Thambi when they met a wise old jackal who listened to both sides of the story.
The jackal advised Thambi to punish the crocodile for its wickedness and suggested using a cudgel to beat it until it was dead.
Thambi punished the crocodile as advised by the jackal.
Thambi returned home to his wife, who had given birth to a beautiful baby boy.
A majestic lion found himself trapped in a rocky cleft, unable to free himself.
A wise and agile horse, who happened to be passing by, overheard the lion's cries for help.
The horse said, 'I'll do that,' but the lion must promise not to devour him once he'd set him free.
The lion, desperate for escape, promised to spare the horse's life.
With a few swift kicks of his hooves, the horse managed to dislodge the rocks and set the lion free.
As soon as the lion was out, he snarled, 'Now that I'm free, I'll make a meal of you!'
The horse retorted, 'What about our agreement? You promised not to eat me.'
They chose the fox as their arbiter and presented their case.
The fox instructed them to return to the rocky cleft, where he would render his verdict.
The lion pushed and squeezed himself back into the narrow space, just as he had been before.
However, with each attempt, he became more wedged in and was finally stuck fast.
The fox declared, 'Now you are exactly where you were before! It's up to the horse whether he'll help you out again.'
But the horse had no intention of doing so; instead, he began hurling stones at the trapped lion.
The fox smiled slyly, knowing that justice had been served.
In a land far and wide, where savannas stretched as far as the eye could see, there lived a mighty lion with a heart as fierce as the sun.
His mane stood tall like a crown, and his roar thundered through the grasslands.
One day, while exploring the rocky outcrops, he found himself trapped in a narrow cleft.
The lion cried 'Ah, wretched fate! Who will set me free?'
A wise horse with a coat as black as coal appeared on the scene.
The lion begged for help, promising not to devour him once freed.
The horse agreed, but only after extracting a promise from the lion.
With his powerful hooves, he dug and dug until the lion was finally set free.
As soon as the lion's paws touched solid ground, he snarled 'Now I'll eat you!'
The horse calmly asked 'What were our conditions?'
The two animals went before a wise fox, who listened carefully to their tale.
The fox asked the lion to push himself in tighter, just as he had been when begging for help.
The lion was wedged in so tightly that he couldn't escape.
The horse hurled stones at the lion until he lay lifeless on the ground.
A mighty lion appeared before a humble farmer, its eyes blazing with an unholy hunger.
The lion demanded one of the farmer's oxen, threatening to slay both beasts and their master if his wish was not granted.
The farmer handed over one of the oxen, buying another to replace it for the next day's work.
The lion returned the next evening, claiming another ox from the farmer's herd.
The farmer surrendered another ox, purchasing yet another to replace it.
