A cunning jackal chanced upon the farmer as he was driving home alone and inquired about the missing oxen.
The jackal outlined a clever ruse to deceive the lion and free the farmer from its threats.
A deep voice boomed from the nearby hill, 'Who speaks with you, farmer?' The lion cowered in fear, stammering that it was only speaking to God.
The farmer replied loudly, 'It is but an Asko!' and swung his hatchet with all his might, splitting the lion's skull in two.
The jackal descended from the hill, a triumphant glint in his eye, declaring that he had fulfilled his promise.
The farmer's wife packed a sheep into a leather sack and placed it alongside their loyal dog in a wicker basket.
When the jackal approached the basket to claim his reward, the dog sprang up, chasing it away with a fierce bark.
The farmer returned to find the ram still intact in the basket and declared that the jackal had failed to claim his prize.
A monarch lived in a far-off kingdom who could understand the language of beasts.
The monarch strolled through his gardens and overheard the fowl praising their benevolent king.
The queen approached the monarch and asked him why he was laughing.
The king hesitated to reveal the true reason for his laughter, fearing it would be a grave mistake.
A cock strutted forward and told the monarch that the queen had bound him to her will.
The queen approached the monarch again, suspecting he was hiding something.
The king proposed building a grand tank for their kingdom, which the queen enthusiastically agreed to.
The animals from Lankawa were gathered at the site to help build the tank, but they struggled with the task.
A jackal observed that there was a peculiar ratio of males to females in their kingdom and offered an explanation for its earlier claim.
The king discovered that the jackal's claim was correct and decided to put an end to the project.
The queen grew enraged when she learned of the king's decision and begged for forgiveness.
The monarch's secret was safe once more, thanks in part to the cunning and wit of the wise jackal.
A mighty king lived in a land where he possessed the gift of conversing with animals.
The king could understand their language after studying it for many moons.
One day, while strolling through his gardens, the king overheard birds discussing his benevolence towards them.
His queen noticed her husband's merriment and asked what had brought him such delight.
The king hesitated to reveal that he understood the birds' conversation, fearing it would be his undoing.
As the days passed, the queen continued to prod her husband for an explanation of his laughter.
The king finally confided in the animals themselves about his secret.
A wise old cock announced that the king was doomed and would no longer receive their assistance unless he perished.
The king's laughter echoed through the land once more, but this time it was tinged with sadness.
A cunning jackal bounded away, leaving the others to their futile labor of building a tank.
The king realized his mistake when he discovered that the males outnumbered the females in the society.
The king called the jackal and acknowledged his wisdom, impressed by his understanding of the balance of their society.
The king tested the jackal's understanding further by asking if they could build a tank, and when they replied it was impossible, he sent them back into the forest.
The queen vowed never to speak or ask anything again after being outwitted by the cunning jackal.
Three poor brothers decided to go hunting for food.
They gathered three ancient guns from the attic, but two were broken and one had no barrel.
The brothers set off into the stormy night, determined to bring back some meat for dinner.
They stumbled upon a lush meadow where a majestic hare was grazing.
The brothers took aim and fired, but their shots missed the mark by a wide margin.
One of the brothers remembered that their wise and benevolent godmother lived in a nearby village.
They knocked on her door, and she offered them three ancient pots from her kitchen, two of which were cracked and one had no bottom.
The brothers chose the pot with no bottom and placed the hare inside it to cook.
One of the brothers suggested that they ask their godmother if she had any fruit trees in her garden.
They picked some walnuts from the barren tree, but one brother broke his heel in the process.
The brothers took the cooked hare and some nuts back to their godmother's house.
They offered free broth to anyone who wanted it, and many villagers flocked to get some.
However, one outsider drank too much of the broth and began to weaken.
The brothers sought out three physicians to find a cure for the ailing stranger.
Three brothers set out on a journey to hunt for sustenance.
Their clothes were tattered, their bellies growling with hunger, but they pressed on, undeterred by the tempests that threatened to consume them.
As they walked, the brothers chanced upon a meadow, where a swift hare darted across their path.
With guns in hand – though two were broken and one had no barrel – they took aim, firing wildly into the air.
The brothers knocked at their godmother's door, and though she was not at home, she answered their plea from afar.
Their godmother told them to enter the kitchen and find three pots, two broken and one with no bottom.
The brothers entered the kitchen and chose the pot without a bottom to cook their elusive hare.
One brother suggested they ask their godmother about her garden, where she had three walnut trees – two dead and one barren of fruit.
The brother who shook the barren tree was rewarded with a nut that fell on his hat, breaking his heel.
In a nearby village, where sickness had taken hold of many hearts, they posted a notice: 'Whoever is hungry, come and receive broth in charity.'
The villagers flocked to partake, but one outsider drank too deeply from the skimmer, and fell into a deep slumber.
The brothers sought out three physicians – one blind, one deaf, and one dumb – to tend to their new friend.

The physicians proved more hindrance than help, with the blind man examining the tongue of the sleeping stranger, the deaf man asking how he was faring, and the dumb man scribbling on a piece of paper.
A mighty dragon dwelled within the depths of the great sea.
The dragon's beloved wife began to wither away, her radiance fading like a flower in autumn's chill.
The dragon asked his wife what he could bring her to eat, as she was not looking well.
The dragon's wife remained silent, her eyes cast downward.
The dragon pleaded with his wife to tell him what she desired, promising to fetch it for her.
The dragon's wife finally spoke, saying that she desired the heart of a monkey to regain her strength.
The dragon set off towards the mountain forests, where the monkeys dwelled.
He spied a clever primate perched high in a tree and called out to him.
The monkey replied that he had no fear of falling from his lofty height.
The dragon offered to carry the monkey on his back across the sea.
As they reached the midpoint, the dragon dived down into the depths of the ocean.
The monkey cried out in alarm, realizing where he was being taken.
The monkey thought quickly and said that he had left his heart on a tree, and asked to be returned to retrieve it.
A mighty dragon dwelled within the depths of the great waters.
The dragon's beloved wife's vitality waned, her radiance fading like the morning sun.
The dragon asked his dear one, 'My love, what shall I bring to ease your suffering?'
His wife remained silent, her eyes downcast.
She whispered that the dragon could not do it and should not trouble himself.
The dragon's heart swelled with devotion as he promised to fulfill his wife's desire.
His wife revealed her heart's longing was for a monkey's heart to eat.
The dragon set off towards the shore to retrieve a monkey's heart.
He spied an agile monkey perched atop a tree and called out to it.
The monkey agreed to cross the sea with the dragon in search of forests teeming with fruit and flowers.
As they descended into the depths, the monkey cried out, 'Where are we going?'
The dragon revealed that his wife was ill and yearned for the monkey's heart.
The monkey realized he had left his heart on the tree and offered to retrieve it for Mrs. Dragon.
The great Buddha spoke to his followers, saying, 'At this time, I was the monkey.'
A prince's parents urged him to find a bride worthy of his station.
The prince refused to settle for any woman unless she was the most sensitive soul in all the land.
His parents told him to go out and seek her himself.
The prince set off on his journey through lush valleys and over misty mountains.
He came across a maiden with a bandaged head, tears streaming down her face.
She explained that her maid had accidentally pulled out a hair while combing her locks, causing her great distress.
The prince thought to himself, 'This is not the one I seek'.
He continued on his way and soon found another woman swathed in fine linens, looking pale and wan.
She replied that a tiny wrinkle in her bedsheet had kept her awake all night, making her feel unwell.
The prince pondered this for a moment before deciding she was not the sensitive one he sought.
He pressed on, seeking wisdom and compassion.
His travels eventually led him to a third maiden, who sat in an elegant chair with a bandaged foot.
Tears streamed down her face as she recounted how a gentle breeze had blown a jasmine blossom petal onto her skin while strolling in the garden.
The prince proposed to her, and she accepted.
In the village of Azizi, where the sun dipped into the horizon like a ripe mango, there lived a prince with a heart full of wonder.
His parents, wise and kind, urged him to find a bride, but he refused, saying, 'I will not marry until I have found a woman whose sensitivity is as vast as the savannah.'
They replied, 'Then go forth and seek her out!'
The prince set off on his journey, his feet carrying him across rivers that flowed like life itself.
He came to a village where women were gathered at the well, their laughter echoing through the air.
Among them was a woman with a bandaged head, her eyes closed in pain.
The prince thought to himself, 'This is not the one I seek. Her sensitivity is as fragile as a reed.'
He continued on his way, passing through fields where the wind whispered secrets to the crops.
He came upon another woman, swathed in fine linens, her face pale as the moon.
The prince thought again, 'This is not the one I seek. Her sensitivity is as delicate as a flower.'
He pressed on, his heart beating like a drum in the distance.
He came to a village where women sat under trees, their voices weaving a tapestry of stories.
Among them was a woman with a bandaged foot, her eyes streaming tears like the rivers that nourished the land.
The prince thought for a moment, and then his heart spoke, 'You are the one I seek. Your sensitivity is as boundless as the sky.'
Sennuccio and his wife Bedovina sat idly by the entrance of their humble abode, reluctant to retire for the night.
Sennuccio told Bedovina to close the door, but she refused, suggesting he shut it himself.
The two continued to bicker until Sennuccio proposed a wager: whoever spoke first would be responsible for shutting the door.
Bedovina agreed to this plan, and they sat in silence, neither one willing to speak first.
Bedovina's eyelids grew heavy with sleep, and she rose from her seat, shedding her garments as she made her way to their bedroom.
A young servant boy passed by, carrying a lantern with a flickering candle, but the flame went out, forcing him to enter Sennuccio's house.
The servant boy spotted Sennuccio sitting motionless on a bench, his eyes wide open but refusing to speak.
The servant boy took Sennuccio by the hand and tried to rouse him, but he remained silent, too afraid of incurring the penalty.
The servant boy then noticed a faint glow emanating from the hearth and entered the inner room, where he found Bedovina lying alone in bed.
The servant boy lay down beside Bedovina, who remained silent throughout, allowing him to do as he pleased while her husband watched from the bench.
When the servant boy departed, Bedovina arose and made her way to the entrance, where she found Sennuccio still awake.
Bedovina rebuked Sennuccio for leaving her lying all night with the door wide open, inviting any rascal to enter their home.
Sennuccio retorted that he had outwitted Bedovina and was now free from shutting the door.
Bedovina realized she had lost the wager and closed the door with a mixture of chagrin and amusement.
In the village of Azizi, where the sun dips into the horizon and paints the sky with hues of crimson and gold, there lived a couple, Sennuccio and Bedovina.
One evening, as they sat near the entrance of their hut, basking in the warmth of the season, Sennuccio said to his wife, 'Bedovina, it is time for us to retire, shut the door.'
But she replied, 'You may shut it yourself, my husband'.
The dispute continued, with neither willing to concede.
Sennuccio proposed a wager: 'Let us make a bargain, Bedovina. The one who speaks first shall shut the door.'
His wife, known for her stubbornness and laziness, agreed to this proposal.
As they sat in silence, the air grew thick with anticipation.
Bedovina soon found herself succumbing to the weight of her eyelids, and she slipped away to bed.
A young servant from the nearby estate passed by their hut, seeking refuge in Sennuccio's open door.
The servant entered further into the house, where he found Sennuccio sitting on a bench, his eyes wide open.
A humble farmer and his wife sat by the fire after supper.
A gust of wind had blown open the door, causing them to bicker over who should close it.
The husband proposed a wager: let the one who spoke first be responsible for shutting the door.
His wife agreed to the arrangement, and they retired to bed in silence.
Later that night, as they lay abed, they heard a strange noise.
They beheld a wild beast, its eyes glowing in the dark, rummaging through their store of provisions.
The creature departed as suddenly as it had appeared.
The next morning, while his wife was away at the mill, the barber chanced upon the farmer and asked how he came to be alone in the house.
The man remained mute, not uttering a syllable.
The barber attempted to rouse him with a shave, but still no words escaped the farmer's lips.
The barber continued his ministrations, shaving off half the farmer's beard and mustache, then covering his face in lampblack.
The barber cried out that the man was bewitched and hastily departed.
The wife returned from her errand to behold her husband transformed into a pitiful sight.
She exclaimed, 'Ah, wretch! What sorcery has befallen thee?'
A poor but proud farmer and his wife sat by the fire in their village home, their bellies full from a meager supper.
The air was thick with the scent of burning wood and simmering emotions as they bickered over who should shut the door that had swung open in the gusty wind.
The farmer said 'Who speaks first gets to close it,' his voice firm but tinged with a hint of desperation.
The wife's eyes flashed like the stars on a clear night as she retorted 'I will not be silenced,' her words dripping with conviction.
A wild dog slipped into their home and devoured the family's meager store of food while they sat in silence.
The next morning, the woman set out to grind grain at her neighbor's mill, leaving the farmer alone in the house.
A barber chanced upon the farmer and exclaimed 'What sorcery is this?' but the farmer remained mute.
The barber shaved off half the farmer's beard and mustache and coated him in lampblack, still the man did not speak.
The wife returned from her errand and saw her husband transformed by his own silence, his face a ghastly mask of stillness.
She cried 'Ah, wretch! What have you done?' trembling with fear.
The farmer's voice cut through the air like a knife as he said 'You spoke first,' his eyes glinting with a hint of triumph.
He ordered her to begone and shut the door, ending their silence.
A man and his wife lived in a small village nestled between two great rivers.
The husband suggested making fritters like his mother used to make for him when he was a child.
His wife asked what they would use to fry the fritters in.
The husband told his wife to ask his godmother if she could borrow her pan.
The wife returned with the pan and reminded the husband that he had to take it back when they were done.
After eating fritters, the couple agreed on a wager: whoever spoke first would have to carry the pan back.
The wife began spinning cotton into thread while humming a gentle tune.
A soldier rode by and asked for directions to a shoemaker in the area.
The soldier dismounted and knocked on their door, asking the husband to cut his horse's girth.
The wife finally broke her silence when she saw her husband's life in danger.
Her husband took advantage of the distraction to say he would take care of cutting the horse's girth.
He won their little wager and cut the horse's girth.
The wife smiled at her husband and said 'sometimes silence is golden'.
A great drought had beset the realm, and the rivers had shrunk to mere trickles.
A grand assembly was convened to ponder the problem of the drought.
One of the creatures suggested dancing upon the dry riverbed to stir up hidden waters.
The assembly agreed to dance, and with great enthusiasm, all began to dance.
As they danced, the earth trembled, and water began to rise to the surface.
Rabbit refused to join in the dance, but later quenched his thirst from the newly revealed waters.
The assembly discovered Rabbit's footprints in the earth and decided to take action against him.
Old Tortoise suggested catching the wily Rabbit by lying down by the water's edge with his shell coated in pitch.
Rabbit trod upon Tortoise, but his feet stuck fast to the pitch-covered shell.
Tortoise slowly turned himself around and began to make his way back to the assembly with Rabbit on his back.
The assembly deliberated seeking a suitable punishment for Rabbit's transgressions.
Rabbit proposed that he be taken by his tail and dashed against a stone as a shameful death.
Lion was chosen to carry out the sentence, but as he swung Rabbit around, the white skin slipped off, leaving behind only a tuft of hair.
A great drought had befallen the creatures of the forest.
A grand assembly was convened to discuss what could be done to bring forth the life-giving waters.
A young and agile Monkey suggested that they all gather at the dry riverbed and dance with reckless abandon.
The other animals laughed and danced with abandon, their paws pounding the dry earth in unison.
Water began to seep from the ground, forming a small pool that grew larger by the minute.
Rabbit slipped away to the riverbed at night and drank his fill from the newly formed pool.
The animals discovered Rabbit's footprints in the mud and realized that he had been drinking from their waters.
Old Tortoise suggested covering his shell with pitch and placing him at the edge of the water to catch Rabbit.
Rabbit stepped on Tortoise's pitch-covered shell, getting his feet stuck fast.
The animals watched in amusement as Rabbit was slowly and surely caught by the cunning old Tortoise.
Rabbit begged to be taken by his tail and dashed against a stone, rather than being killed in a shameful manner.
Lion grasped Rabbit's tail and swung him around, but the white skin on Rabbit's back slipped off, leaving nothing but a tuft of hair.
Rabbit was free once more, and the assembly erupted into laughter at the absurdity of it all.
Herr Hase's home was repeatedly raided by Herr Wolf.
Each time, one of his young ones fell prey to the marauder's cruelty.
The rabbit's straw hut was torn asunder, then his pine-top abode, followed by his bark house.
Herr Hase stormed off, vowing to outwit the wolf once and for all.
He sought out skilled craftsmen, who built him a sturdy plank house with a solid rock foundation.
The rabbit's new home stood firm against any assault, and Herr Hase was finally able to enjoy some respite from his troubles.
Herr Wolf observed the rabbit's new stronghold and laid low for a while.
One afternoon, Herr Wolf burst into the house, mud-caked and panting, crying 'Oh, please, Herr Hase! Save me!'
Herr Hase feigned concern and directed the wolf to jump into a nearby chest.
The rabbit then strolled over to the mirror, winked at his reflection, and settled into his favorite rocking chair by the fire.
Herr Hase began to ponder the wolf's predicament and decided to 'make him a nice cup of tea'.
As he bored holes in the chest lid with his gimlet, Herr Wolf's eyes grew wide with alarm.
Herr Hase poured scalding water onto the chest lid, and the wolf's cries were drowned out by the sound of his own skin sizzling.
The wolf succumbed to the scalding water, and Herr Hase summoned his neighbors to celebrate long into the night.
A quick-witted rabbit named Kaito lived in a small clearing surrounded by tall trees.
Kaito's family was often targeted by the wicked wolf, Ryūjin, who delighted in causing trouble for them.
Ryūjin would destroy Kaito's homes and take one of his children with him each time.
Kaito grew tired of this game and decided to take matters into his own hands.
He gathered skilled craftsmen and built a sturdy plank house on a solid rock foundation with a hidden cellar for safety.
Ryūjin tried to destroy the new home but was thwarted by its design and strong construction.
One day, Ryūjin came to Kaito's house, covered in mud and panting heavily, claiming the dogs were after him.
Kaito feigned kindness and ushered Ryūjin into a nearby chest, shutting the lid tight.
Kaito tended to Ryūjin's needs while secretly plotting his next move, all while the little rabbits peeked out from their hiding place and stifled their giggles.
Kaito poured hot water onto the chest lid, scalding Ryūjin through the holes he had bored earlier.
Ryūjin cried out in pain, but it was too late, and Kaito called in his neighbors to celebrate their victory.
From that day on, Ryūjin's hide hung proudly on the back porch of Kaito's house as a reminder of the cunning rabbit's cleverness.
A youth named Hlakanyana chanced upon a young lad tending to a flock of goats.
Hlakanyana proposed that they should jointly pursue after birds for it was a grand pastime.
They chased the feathered creatures throughout the day.
As the sun began to set, Hlakanyana declared 'The time has come to roast our quarry'.
Hlakanyana proposed a game where they would see who emerged from beneath the water's surface last.
They both plunged into the depths of the river, but Hlakanyana cheated and emerged first.
Hlakanyana devoured all the birds, leaving naught but their heads behind.
The boy protested his innocence, but Hlakanyana would not be swayed and demanded the digging-stick as payment.
Hlakanyana lent the artisans his digging-stick to make their work easier.
The digging-stick broke into pieces when the artisans used it, and they offered Hlakanyana a pot in reparation.
Hlakanyana lent the boys his pot to suck milk from their goats, but it shattered into pieces.
The boys offered Hlakanyana a goat in reparation for breaking the pot.
Hlakanyana lent the keepers of calves his goat to suck milk from their charges, but it perished.
The keepers offered Hlakanyana a calf in reparation for killing the goat, and this pattern continued with each group offering more valuable items as reparation.
Hlakanyana chanced upon a young shepherd tending to his flock of goats.
The pair went on an impromptu hunt for birds with Hlakanyana's digging-stick.
They arrived at a tranquil spot on the riverbank and decided to dive into the water to see who would emerge last.
Hlakanyana emerged first from the water, leaving the boy to struggle free.
The boy denied eating their catch, but Hlakanyana insisted that he must pay for the lost meal with the digging-stick.
Hlakanyana gave the digging-stick to a group of artisans who were crafting clay vessels by the river's edge.
The potters accepted the gift but broke the tool in two while using it for their labor.
Hlakanyana retrieved the broken remains and arrived at a clearing where boys were herding goats.
He introduced himself to the young shepherds and handed over a clay container he possessed, which they used but broke.
The boys offered Hlakanyana a goat as compensation for the broken pot.
Hlakanyana proposed that the keepers of calves allow their calf to suckle first to induce milk production in the cows.
The calf perished under the care of the keepers, and Hlakanyana accused them of killing it.
The cowherds presented Hlakanyana with a majestic bull as compensation for the broken tools and lost livestock.
A humble hermit named Kaito wandered through the scorching desert of the Golden Sands.
Kaito stumbled upon a hidden cave and felt an inexplicable sense of unease.
He entered the cave and beheld a treasure trove of gold emanating a radiant glow.
Kaito fled the cave with all haste, his heart racing with an unseen terror.
Three rough men emerged from their hiding place and gave chase to Kaito.
The bandits demanded that Kaito show them 'Death' that pursued him.
Kaito led the bandits to the cave and pointed to the gold, saying it was what he fled from.
The bandits beheld the treasure and congratulated each other on their good fortune.
One of the bandits suggested dividing the treasure among themselves, while another proposed sending one to sell some gold in the city.
The bandit who was sent to the city secretly laced food and drink with poison, intending to eliminate his comrades.
The remaining two bandits awaited their comrade's return, planning to strike him down and then feast on the treasure.
The poisoned food was brought back to the cave, and the three men fell upon each other with lances and knives.
Kaito returned to the cave and beheld the scene of the three lifeless bodies surrounded by the treasure.
The hermit smiled knowingly, having been saved from their fate due to his wisdom and humility.
A wise hermit roamed the dunes with purpose.
He stumbled upon a cave hidden by shifting sands.
The hermit saw a glint of gold in one corner of the cave.
Instead of taking the gold, he fled with all haste.
Three rough men lurked nearby, preying on travelers.
They watched the hermit flee without pursuit and exchanged curious glances.
The three men asked the hermit to lead them to whatever was chasing him.
The hermit warned them not to follow him into the cave, but they ignored his warning.
The men saw a pile of glittering metal in the corner where the gold shone brightly.
They began to discuss how to divide the treasure among themselves.
One of them suggested sending one man to the city to sell some of the gold and bring back supplies.
The poisoned food was brought back to the cave, and the men devoured it, unaware of their fate.
The three men died in each other's arms, their greed and treachery ultimately leading them to a fate worse than death itself.
A devoted husband and wife lived in a small village nestled between two great mountains.
The couple was both skilled artisans, with the husband being a master tailor and his wife a talented seamstress and expert housekeeper.
Their tidy home was disrupted by a series of unfortunate events: pots, glasses, and plates lay shattered on the kitchen floor.
The husband questioned his wife about the destruction, asking 'Who broke these precious items?'
His wife replied, 'I did it, with my scissors.'
The husband demanded to know if she was telling the truth.
He tied a sturdy rope around her waist and began to lower her into their well, saying 'Come, let us see if it was truly your scissors that caused this destruction.'
As he lowered her deeper into the well, his wife continued to maintain that she had used the scissors.
The husband's patience wore thin as he asked her again and again what she had done with the broken items.
His wife's obstinacy only grew stronger until finally, her feet touched the cool water at the bottom of the well.
The husband let go of the rope, and his wife slipped beneath the surface of the water.
As she struggled to breathe, she continued to insist that it was indeed the scissors that had broken the household items.
The husband pulled her out of the well, hoping that she might finally reveal what had really happened.
Even after being lifted out of the water, his wife still refused to confess.
There was a tale told of a ship doomed to sail the seven seas for eternity, called the Flying Dutchman.
The Dutchman was cursed by the gods themselves.
In winter months, no ship dared enter the treacherous waters of the Cape of Good Hope.
A stout galleon from Batavia struggled to make port at Table Bay despite the treacherous conditions.
The batteries forced the ship to put back out to sea, where it was lost in the maelstrom.
Many have claimed to have seen the Dutchman sailing through the Cape of Good Hope with all sail set and her crew scrambling to keep her upright.
The narrator himself witnessed the Dutchman on a voyage from Algoa to Simon's Bay many years ago.
Captain Owen attempted to close with the phantom vessel but she stood away, leaving them to wonder at her strange behavior.
The next morning, they anchored in Simon's Bay and found that the Barracouta had been 300 miles from them when they thought they saw the Dutchman.
Many years ago, on a voyage from England to India, the narrator witnessed another apparition of the Flying Dutchman.
The Dutchman seemed to be bearing down upon them with all sail set as if to speak with them, but vanished into darkness in an instant.
The narrator swears that he has seen the wretched vessel with his own eyes and knows it is real.
It is said that any vessel which the Dutchman can get her letters on board of is certain to be lost forevermore.
A humble farmer named Kaito lived in a small village nestled between two great mountains.
A fearsome lion appeared, demanding one of Kaito's precious oxen as tribute each evening.
Kaito reluctantly surrendered an ox each night to the lion.
A cunning jackal named Jiro devised a plan to free Kaito from the lion's tyranny.
Jiro proposed that in exchange for ridding him of the lion, he would be rewarded with a sheep.
Kaito agreed to Jiro's proposal and was instructed on how to outwit the lion.
The next day, the lion asked who was speaking with Kaito, but Kaito boldly declared it was only an Asko (a block of wood).
Kaito split the lion's skull with his hatchet, ending its reign of terror.
Jiro descended from the hill, triumphant in his promise to rid Kaito of the lion.
Kaito's wife whispered a mischievous plan into his ear regarding Jiro's reward.
The next day, Kaito presented Jiro with a wicker basket containing a leather sack with the promised ram inside.
However, their clever dog had been placed beside it, and when Jiro approached to claim his prize, the canine sprang up, chasing the jackal away.
Kaito realized that Jiro had not claimed his reward after all and decided to enjoy the ram themselves.
Ah-Ming and his wife Mei-Ling lived in a small village nestled between two great mountains.
Ah-Ming said to Mei-Ling, 'Let us make some delicious yóu tiáo' – crispy fried dough sticks – for our evening meal.
Mei-Ling replied, 'But where shall we cook them? We have no wok.'
Ah-Ming suggested, 'Go and borrow one from my jiaojie, my aunt by marriage. It is only a short walk away.'
Mei-Ling went to her aunt's house and borrowed the wok.
After finishing their meal, Ah-Ming said, 'Now let us go to work, both of us, and the one who speaks first shall carry the wok back to your aunt's house.'
A lone soldier rode by on his horse, asking a passerby if there was a skilled shoemaker in the neighborhood.
The woman pointed to their house and said, 'Ah, yes! That is where Ah-Ming lives.'
The soldier dismounted and entered their home, saying, 'Good fellow, I need you to cut a new girth for my horse. I will pay you well.'
Mei-Ling cried out in distress, 'Ah! Please don't harm him!' when the soldier threatened Ah-Ming.
A man and his wife, both skilled tailors, lived in harmony in a small village.
Disaster struck when their kitchen's pots, glasses, and plates were shattered on the floor.
The husband surveyed the damage and asked his wife how it happened.
His wife replied that she didn't know, her voice barely above a whisper.
The husband's patience wore thin as he accused his wife of breaking the items.
The wife took responsibility for the damage with the scissors, daring him to contradict her.
The husband tied a rope around his wife's waist and began lowering her into the well nearby.
As they descended deeper into the well, the wife continued to insist that it was indeed the scissors that had shattered the items.
The husband released the rope, and his wife plunged into the water, but her hand shot up out of the water like scissors cutting through air.
The husband pulled his wife to safety as they emerged from the well, dripping wet and shivering with fear.
The villagers gathered around, whispering among themselves as they watched the couple.
One of them said, 'A stubborn heart is like a stone,' shaking her head in dismay.
Another added, 'Sometimes, it takes more than words to reveal the truth.'
